title: Apply: connecting motion and forces
description: Apply your understanding of forces and acceleration in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students places a fan cart at one end of a track.  When the fan is switched on, a constant force is exerted on the cart. The greater the fan’s speed, the stronger the force. The students can add or remove blocks to change the cart's mass. Their setup is modeled below.

[[☃ image 3]]

The students switch on the fan and release the cart. They measure the time it takes to reach the other end. The first time they try this, the cart moves very fast. 

**Which of the following changes would decrease the cart’s acceleration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An object’s acceleration depends on two factors:

* the object's mass
* the net force acting on the object

The cart's mass includes any blocks on top of it. The net force acting on the fan cart is the force of the air on the fan.
For the same net force applied, a more massive object will experience a smaller acceleration. Adding more blocks to the cart will increase its mass, and therefore decrease its acceleration.
For the same mass object, a weaker net force will cause a smaller acceleration. Decreasing the fan's speed will make the net force weaker, and therefore decrease the acceleration.

The answers are:

* add more blocks to the cart
* decrease the fan's speed

options.alt: A track sits on top of a table. A cart is at the right end of the track. The cart has a fan attached on top of it which blows to the right. Two blocks sit on top of the cart. Five additional blocks sit on the tabletop to the right of the track.

choices.content: add more blocks to the cart
take some blocks off the cart

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students places a fan cart at one end of a track.  When the fan is switched on, a constant force is exerted on the cart. The greater the fan’s speed, the stronger the force. The students can add or remove blocks to change the cart's mass. Their setup is modeled below.

[[☃ image 3]]

The students switch on the fan and release the cart. They measure the time it takes to reach the other end. The first time they try this, the cart moves very slowly.

**Which of the following changes would increase the cart’s acceleration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An object’s acceleration depends on two factors:

* the object's mass
* the net force acting on the object

The cart's mass includes any blocks on top of it. The net force acting on the fan cart is the force of the air on the fan.
For the same net force applied, a less massive object will experience a larger acceleration. Removing some blocks from the cart will decrease its mass, and therefore increase its acceleration.
For the same mass object, a stronger net force will cause a larger acceleration. Increasing the fan's speed will make the net force stronger, and therefore increase the acceleration.

The answers are:

* take some blocks off the cart
* increase the fan's speed

options.alt: A track sits on top of a table. A cart is at the right end of the track. The cart has a fan attached on top of it which blows to the right. Two blocks sit on top of the cart. Five additional blocks sit on the tabletop to the right of the track.

choices.content: add more blocks to the cart
take some blocks off the cart

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students places a fan cart at one end of a track.  When the fan is switched on, a constant force is exerted on the cart. The greater the fan’s speed, the stronger the force. The students can add or remove blocks to change the cart's mass. Their setup is modeled below.

 [[☃ image 3]]

The students switch on the fan and release the cart. They measure the time it takes to reach the other end. The first time they try this, the cart moves very fast.

**Complete the statement.**

* To decrease the cart's acceleration, the students could  [[☃ dropdown 1]] the cart or [[☃ dropdown 2]] the fan's speed.
hints:
An object’s acceleration depends on two factors:

* the object's mass
* the net force acting on the object

The cart's mass includes any blocks on top of it. The net force acting on the fan cart is the force of the air on the fan.
For the same net force applied, a more massive object will experience a smaller acceleration. Adding more blocks to the cart will increase its mass, and therefore decrease its acceleration.
For the same mass object, a weaker net force will cause a smaller acceleration. Decreasing the fan's speed will make the net force weaker, and therefore decrease the acceleration.

The answer is:

* To decrease the cart's acceleration, the students could add some blocks to the cart or decrease the fan's speed.

options.alt: A track sits on top of a table. A cart is at the right end of the track. The cart has a fan attached on top of it which blows to the right. Two blocks sit on top of the cart. Five additional blocks sit on the tabletop to the right of the track.

assessment_items.question_content:
For fun, people drive **bumper cars** designed to safely collide and bounce off each other.

 [[☃ image 1]]

Two identical cars collide head-on. Each car is carrying one driver. During the collision, car A’s velocity changes more than car B’s velocity does.

**What must be the reason car A experiences a greater acceleration than car B?**  
*Assume the only horizontal forces acting on the cars are those they exert on each other.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When two objects interact, each exerts a force on the other. These forces are always *equal in strength.* So, car A can’t exert a stronger or weaker force on car B than B does on A.

An object’s acceleration depends on its mass and the net force that acts on it. We’re told no horizontal forces act on the cars except those they exert on each other. So, the net force each car experiences is the force from the other car. 

Cars A and B exert the same strength force on each other. So, they experience the same strength net force during the collision.

When two objects experience the same strength net force, the one with the smaller mass will have the greater acceleration.
Car A has a greater acceleration (its velocity changes more) than B. So, it must have a smaller mass. 

Since the cars are identical, this difference in mass must be due to a difference in mass of the drivers.

The answer is:

car A’s driver is less massive than car B’s driver

options.alt: Two bumper cars each carry a pair of smiling riders.

choices.content: car A’s driver is less massive than car B’s driver
car A’s driver is more massive than car B’s driver
car A exerts a stronger force on car B than B exerts on A
car A exerts a weaker force on car B than B exerts on A

assessment_items.question_content:
For fun, people drive **bumper cars** designed to safely collide and bounce off each other.

 [[☃ image 1]]

Two identical cars collide head-on. Each car is carrying one driver. During the collision, car A experiences a greater acceleration than car B does.

**Complete the statements.**  
*Assume the only horizontal forces acting on the cars are those they exert on each other.*

* During the collision, the strength of the force from car A on car B is [[☃ dropdown 1]] the force from car B on car A. 
* The mass of car A's driver is [[☃ dropdown 2]] car B's driver.
hints: When two objects interact, each exerts a force on the other. These forces are always *equal in strength.* So, the strength of the force car A  exerts on car B must be *equal to* the force car B exerts on car A.

An object’s acceleration depends on its mass and the net force that acts on it. We’re told no horizontal forces act on the cars except those they exert on each other. So, the net force each car experiences is the force from the other car. 

Cars A and B exert the same strength force on each other. So, they experience the same strength net force during the collision.

When two objects experience the same strength net force, the one with the smaller mass will have the greater acceleration.
Car A has a greater acceleration than B. So, it must have a smaller mass. 

Since the cars are identical, this difference in mass must be due to a difference in mass of the drivers.

The answer is:

* During the collision, the strength of the force from car A on car B is equal to the force from car B on car A. 
* The mass of car A's driver is less than car B's driver.

options.alt: Two bumper cars each carry a pair of smiling riders.

assessment_items.question_content:
For fun, people drive **bumper cars** designed to safely collide and bounce off each other.

 [[☃ image 1]]

Two identical cars collide head-on. Each car is carrying one driver. During the collision, car A’s velocity changes less than car B’s velocity does.

**What must be the reason car A experiences a smaller acceleration than car B?**  
*Assume the only horizontal forces acting on the cars are those they exert on each other.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When two objects interact, each exerts a force on the other. These forces are always *equal in strength.* So, car A can’t exert a stronger or weaker force on car B than B does on A.

An object’s acceleration depends on its mass and the net force that acts on it. We’re told no horizontal forces act on the cars except those they exert on each other. So, the net force each car experiences is the force from the other car. 

Cars A and B exert the same strength force on each other. So, they experience the same strength net force during the collision.

When two objects experience the same strength net force, the one with the larger mass will have the smaller acceleration.
Car A has a smaller acceleration (its velocity changes less) than B. So, it must have a larger mass. 

Since the cars are identical, this difference in mass must be due to a difference in mass of the drivers.

The answer is:

car A’s driver is more massive than car B’s driver

options.alt: Two bumper cars each carry a pair of smiling riders.

choices.content: car A exerts a weaker force on car B than B exerts on A
car A exerts a stronger force on car B than B exerts on A
car A’s driver is less massive than car B’s driver
car A’s driver is more massive than car B’s driver

assessment_items.question_content:
Three trucks start at rest, side-by-side. The trucks carry different mass loads. The three drivers press their accelerators at the same time, and the trucks experience equal strength net forces. The positions of the three trucks before starting and a short time later are modeled below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Rank the mass of each truck (including its load) from greatest to least.**

*greatest mass* 

[[☃ sorter 1]] 

*least mass*
hints: If a net force acts on an object, the object will accelerate. For the same net force, a more massive object will experience a smaller acceleration.
The same net force acts on all three trucks. So, to rank their masses from greatest to least, we should rank their accelerations from least to greatest.

Acceleration is change in velocity over time. 

All three trucks started at rest. All are shown again after experiencing the same net force for the same amount of time. The farther a truck travelled in that time, the greater its acceleration must have been.
Ranking the trucks’ accelerations, truck **G** has the smallest acceleration, followed by truck **B**. Truck **R** has the largest acceleration.

The answer is:

*greatest mass*  

**G**

**B**

**R**

*least mass*

options.alt: A top view of a road with three lanes going left to right. Three trucks are aligned at the left edge of the road with their fronts at a dotted line labeled start.  The top truck is green and is labeled G. The middle truck is red and is labeled R. The bottom truck is blue and is labeled B.  To the right of the dotted line, a second image of each truck is shown. The green truck is closest to the starting line. The blue truck is farther. The red truck is the farthest from the starting line. The three trucks each show motion lines behind them, with the red truck's lines being the longest and the green truck's lines the shortest.

assessment_items.question_content:
Three identical trucks start at rest, side-by-side. The three drivers press their accelerators at the same time, but by different amounts. So, the trucks experience different strength net forces. The positions of the three trucks before starting and a short time later are modeled below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Rank the net force experienced by each truck from strongest to weakest.**

*strongest net force* 

[[☃ sorter 1]] 

*weakest net force*
hints: If a net force acts on an object, the object will accelerate. For objects with the same mass, a greater net force applied will cause a greater acceleration.

The trucks are identical, which means they have the same mass. So, to rank the strengths of the net forces they experience from greatest to least, we should rank their accelerations from greatest to least.

Acceleration is change in velocity over time. 

All three trucks started at rest. All are shown again after experiencing the same net force for the same amount of time. The farther a truck travelled in that time, the greater its acceleration must have been.
Ranking the trucks’ accelerations, truck **R** has the greatest acceleration, followed by truck **B**. Truck **G** has the smallest acceleration.

The answer is:

*strongest net force* 

**R**

**B**

**G**

*weakest net force*

options.alt: A top view of a road with three lanes going left to right. Three trucks are aligned at the left edge of the road with their fronts at a dotted line labeled start.  The top truck is green and is labeled G. The middle truck is red and is labeled R. The bottom truck is blue and is labeled B.  To the right of the dotted line, a second image of each truck is shown. The green truck is closest to the starting line. The blue truck is farther. The red truck is the farthest from the starting line. The three trucks each show motion lines behind them, with the red truck's lines being the longest and the green truck's lines the shortest.

**G**

assessment_items.question_content:
A student applies a $1.0\,\text{N}$ net force to a $1.0\,\text{kg}$ cart. Another student measures its acceleration with a motion sensor. The cart’s acceleration is $1.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$ A model of the experiment is shown below.

[[☃ image 3]]

The students repeat their experiment for a total of four trials, using different combinations of mass and net force.  Their data table is below.

trial number | net force $(\text{N})$ | mass $(\text{kg})$ | acceleration $\left(\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\right)$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-: 
$1$ | $1.0$ | $1.0$ | $1.0$
$2$ | $1.0$ | $2.0$ | $0.5$
$3$ | $2.0$ | $1.0$ | $2.0$
$4$ | $2.0$ | $2.0$ | $1.0$

**Based on the data, which equation could be a model for how acceleration is related to net force and mass?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A horizontal track has a device at the left end that looks like a speaker pointed horizontally to the right. It is labeled motion sensor. In the middle of the track is a cart with three lines to the left of it, representing motion to the right. A hook on the right end of the cart is connected to a hook on a rectangular device labeled force sensor. A hand holds the right end of the force sensor so that it is parallel to the track. The force sensor has a display that reads 1.0 N.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student uses a force sensor to apply a constant net force to a $2.0\,\text{kg}$ cart. Another student measures its acceleration with a motion sensor. A model of the experiment is shown below.

[[☃ image 3]]

The students repeat their experiment multiple times. They use the same cart but apply different strength net forces. They graph their data, which models how the cart's acceleration changes as the net force changes. 

[[☃ image 6]]

**Analyze the graph to complete the statements.**

* When the net force doubles, the acceleration [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* If the force is increased to $6\,\text{N},$ the acceleration will be [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$
hints:
When a value is doubled, it's multiplied by $2.$ So, if we double the smallest force plotted on the graph $(1.0\,\text{N})$ we get:

$1.0\,\text{N}\times2=2.0\,\text{N}$

Let's compare the acceleration when the force is $1.0\,\text{N}$ to the acceleration when the force is $2.0\,\text{N}.$

[[☃ image 2]]

When the force is $\color{#0000ff}1.0\,\text{N}\color{#000000},$ the acceleration is $\color{#0000ff}0.5\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\color{#000000}.$

When the force is $\color{#ff0000}2.0\,\text{N}\color{000000},$ the acceleration is $\color{#ff0000}1.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\color{#000000}.$

An acceleration of $\color{#ff0000}1.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}$ is *two times* larger than $\color{#0000ff}0.5\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$ 

So, when the net force doubled, the acceleration also *doubled*.

Now we need the cart's acceleration if the force is increased to $6\,\text{N}.$  

We determined that when the force doubles, the acceleration doubles. So, the acceleration with a force of $6\,\text{N}$ will be $2$ *times* the acceleration when the force is $3\,\text{N}.$

[[☃ image 1]]

From the graph we see that when the force is $\color{darkorange}{3\,\text{N}}\color{black}{,}$ the acceleration is $\color{darkorange}1.5\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\color{black}{.}$ If we double this we get:

$1.5\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\times2=\boxed{3.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}}$

The answers are:

* When the net force doubles, the acceleration doubles.

* If the force is increased to $6\,\text{N},$ the acceleration will be $3.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$

options.alt: A horizontal track has a device at the left end that looks like a speaker pointed horizontally to the right. It is labeled motion sensor. In the middle of the track is a cart with three lines to the left of it, representing motion to the right. A hook on the right end of the cart is connected to a hook on a rectangular device labeled force sensor. A hand holds the right end of the force sensor so that it is parallel to the track. The force sensor has a display that reads 1.0 N.

A graph shows acceleration on the y-axis and net force on the x-axis. Five points are plotted: 
- When the force is 0 newtons, the acceleration is 0 meters per second squared. 
- When the force is 1 newton, the acceleration is 0.5 meters per second squared. 
- When the force is 2 newtons, the acceleration is 1 meter per second squared. 
- When the force is 3 newtons, the acceleration is 1.5 meters per second squared. 
- When the force is 4 newtons, the acceleration is 2 meters per second squared. 
The graphed points are connected by a straight line that slants up and to the right.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student uses a force sensor to apply a constant net force of $2.0\,\text{N}$ to a cart. Another student measures its acceleration with a motion sensor. A model of the experiment is shown below.

[[☃ image 7]]

The students repeat their experiment multiple times. They apply the same $2.0\,\text{N}$ net force each time, but add blocks to the cart to change its mass. They graph their data, which models how the cart's acceleration changes as its mass changes. 

[[☃ image 8]]

**Analyze the graph to complete the statements.**

* When the mass is doubled, the acceleration [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* If the mass is increased to $8\,\text{kg},$ the acceleration will be [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$
hints:
When a value is doubled, it's multiplied by $2.$ So, if we double the smallest mass plotted on the graph $(1.0\,\text{kg})$ we get:

$1.0\,\text{kg}\times2=2.0\,\text{kg}$

Let's compare the acceleration when the mass is $1.0\,\text{kg}$ with the acceleration when the mass is $2.0\,\text{kg}.$

[[☃ image 3]]

When the mass is $\color{#0000ff}1.0\,\text{kg}\color{#000000},$ the acceleration is $\color{#0000ff}2.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\color{#000000}.$

When the mass is $\color{#ff0000}2.0\,\text{kg}\color{000000},$ the acceleration is $\color{#ff0000}1.0\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\color{#000000}.$

An acceleration of $\color{#ff0000}1.0\,\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}$ is *half* of $\color{#0000ff}2.0\,\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$ 

So, when the mass doubled, the acceleration was *cut in half*.

Now we need the cart's acceleration if the mass is increased to $8\,\text{kg}.$  

When the mass doubles, the acceleration is cut in half. So, the acceleration for a mass of $8\,\text{kg}$ will be *half* of what it was when the mass was $4\,\text{kg}.$

[[☃ image 2]]

From the graph, when the mass is $\color{darkorange}4\,\text{kg}\color{black}{,}$ the acceleration is $\color{darkorange}0.5\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}\color{black}{.}$ If we cut this in half (divide by $2$) we get:

$\dfrac{0.5\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}}{2}=\boxed{0.25\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}}$

The answers are:

* When the mass doubles, the acceleration is cut in half.

* If the mass is increased to $8\,\text{kg},$ the acceleration will be $0.25\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.$

options.alt: A horizontal track has a device at the left end that looks like a speaker pointed horizontally to the right. It is labeled motion sensor. In the middle of the track is a cart with three lines to the left of it, representing motion to the right. A hook on the right end of the cart is connected to a hook on a rectangular device labeled force sensor. A hand holds the right end of the force sensor so that it is parallel to the track. The force sensor has a display that reads 2.0 N.

A graph shows acceleration on the y-axis and mass on the x-axis. Four points are plotted: 
- When the mass is 1 kilogram, the acceleration is 2 meters per second squared. 
- When the mass is 2 kilograms, the acceleration is 1 meters per second squared. 
- When the mass is 3 kilograms, the acceleration is about 0.7 meters per second squared. 
- When the mass is 4 kilograms, the acceleration is 0.5 meters per second squared. 
The graphed points are connected by a curved line that curves down and to the right, like the left side of a bowl.
